title: Collections log page layout
subtitle: Designing a page to catalogue a collection by hand
excerpt: A hand-ruled collections log page is more useful than a plain notebook entry because the layout forces you to record the same categories of information each time. Whether cataloguing seeds, fabrics, minerals, or printed ephemera, a consistent grid means that a collection log from three years ago can be compared directly to today's.
sourceNotes: Collections log layout guidance synthesised from journalling and cataloguing practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hand-ruled grid of consistent cells on a journal page, each cell large enough for a thumbnail sketch, a sample swatch, or a short text entry. The grid provides equal visual weight to each catalogued item.

content.text: The design of a collections log page depends on two decisions: how many items fit on a page, and what information each item needs. Both decisions are made before ruling begins. The result is an

of consistent cells, each holding the same categories of data. For a seed collection, an entry might need: seed name, source, date, and a small drawn image. Work out the field list first, then design the cell to contain it.

Sketch a single entry cell on scrap paper first. A cell for a moderately detailed record, say, four to five lines of text plus a 40 mm square sketch area, works at 90 × 60 mm on a landscape page. Three cells per row, two rows per page gives six entries per spread at that size. A simpler record (name, date, one line of notes) can be smaller, fitting eight or ten entries per page.

Measure and mark the cell positions in light pencil. Rule the grid lines with a metal ruler and a fine pen or a 0.3 mm pigment liner. A header bar across the top of each cell (5, 8 mm high) for the entry number or name, ruled with a slightly heavier line than the cell body, helps the eye separate the header from the content at a glance.

A single thin watercolour wash in the header bar, a consistent colour per collection type, adds visual organisation without much work. Brush a strip of dilute colour across each header bar and let dry before ruling over it. If you use a rubber stamp for page headers or category labels, stamp first, then rule: ruling over a stamped area risks smearing the ink.

Once you have a cell design you are satisfied with, rule a template page on a spare sheet. Keep it in the journal as the first page, and trace the cell positions onto new pages by holding them up to the light or using the light box. This is faster than re-measuring every time and keeps the grid consistent through the journal.
